title: Condense map hover popup and keep it fully inside the map viewport
description:
Tighten up the map mouseover/details popup so it is more condensed and never overflows the visible map area.

In the captured example, the popup is a dark rounded aircraft card showing:
- `Aircraft` type pill
- `1s ago` in the upper-right
- close `x` button
- aircraft identifier `AFR69ZJ`
- hex/ICAO-ish identifier `3985A4`
- metric cards for `Speed 402 kt`, `Heading 209°`, and `Altitude 34975 ft`
- a `CALLSIGN` section showing `AFR69ZJ`
- the next section appears to be `FEED`, but the bottom of the popup is clipped before the full card can be seen

The core UX problem is that the popup is too tall/roomy and can extend beyond the map container, so the full content cannot always be seen while hovering/selecting tracks.
implementation:
# Goal
Make the map details popup more space-efficient and viewport-aware so the full popup remains visible inside the map area.

# Required behavior
- Reduce the visual height/footprint of the hover/details popup.
- Ensure the popup never renders outside the visible map container.
- Keep the key information readable and quickly scannable.
- Handle edge positions near the bottom/top/sides of the map gracefully.

# Screenshot details captured in issue
The example popup currently contains:
- `Aircraft` badge
- `1s ago`
- close button
- main identifier `AFR69ZJ`
- secondary identifier `3985A4`
- stat blocks for `Speed 402 kt`, `Heading 209°`, `Altitude 34975 ft`
- `CALLSIGN: AFR69ZJ`
- a partially visible `FEED` section
The popup is clipped at the bottom, so the full content cannot be seen inside the map.

# Design notes
- Review whether this is a hover popup, selected-marker popup, or shared component and update the common component if appropriate.
- Prefer reducing wasted vertical space first: padding, gaps, heading sizes, and oversized stat cards.
- Add viewport-aware positioning so the popup can flip or shift when near map edges.
- If needed, apply a max-height relative to the map viewport and allow internal scrolling only as a fallback, not the first choice.
- Preserve legibility on both desktop and smaller viewport sizes.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Audit the current popup component/CSS and how MapLibre positions it.
2. Condense the card layout and spacing.
3. Add viewport-boundary handling so the popup repositions or flips instead of overflowing.
4. Add guardrails for very small map areas or unusually long content.
5. Test with aircraft/vessel entries near all map edges and corners.

# Acceptance criteria
- The popup is visibly more compact than the current version.
- Key fields remain readable.
- The full popup stays inside the map viewport in edge/corner cases.
- The bottom of the card is no longer clipped like in the captured example.

title: Update deployment docs and README for current auth and runtime setup
description:
Update the deployment documentation and the project README so they reflect the current runtime/deployment story clearly.

This should cover the deployment setup, runtime configuration, and any new authentication-related behavior that affects operators or self-hosters. The README should give a concise, accurate overview and quick-start guidance, while the deployment docs should contain the fuller operational details.
close_reason: Synchronized README and deployment docs with current auth/runtime behavior
implementation:
# Goal
Bring the user-facing project documentation back in sync with the actual deployment and runtime behavior, with a clear split between README overview material and detailed deployment documentation.

# Required behavior
- Update the README with an accurate project summary, local/dev quick-start, and deployment pointers.
- Update deployment docs with the current runtime configuration and operator workflow.
- Document public vs authenticated/private mode behavior where relevant.
- Document any required configuration for auth/bootstrap users/JWT or gRPC once those pieces land.
- Ensure README and deployment docs do not drift or contradict each other.

# Design notes
- Keep the README concise and oriented toward first contact: what Hrafnsyn is, key capabilities, local startup path, and where to find deeper docs.
- Keep detailed operational instructions in `docs/` and deployment-specific material in the Nix/deployment documentation.
- Cross-check docs against `config/runtime.exs`, `nix/module.nix`, and the actual app behavior instead of documenting intended behavior only.
- Treat this as documentation synchronization work that may need to be revisited as the auth and gRPC issues are implemented.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Audit the current README and deployment docs for stale or missing guidance.
2. Update the README overview, setup, and deployment pointers.
3. Update deployment documentation for runtime config and operational expectations.
4. Add or refresh auth-related deployment guidance as private/public mode support evolves.
5. Cross-check the docs against runtime config, Nix options, and actual startup flow.

# Acceptance criteria
- README gives an accurate quick introduction and points to the right deeper docs.
- Deployment documentation reflects the current runtime and operator setup.
- Auth/deployment guidance is documented consistently once the underlying features exist.
- Documentation references are internally consistent and not stale.

title: Add web UI for users and admins to view, issue, and revoke API tokens
description:
Add a web UI so users can see which API tokens they have, revoke their own tokens, and issue a new token from the app. Admins should also be able to inspect and manage the broader token issue set, including global/admin-level token revocation capabilities.

This should live under the far-right account/admin dropdown in the header.

In the captured header example, the top navigation shows:
- `Live map`
- `gRPC API`
- `Admin`
- a far-right account control showing `admin`, `Admin`, and an avatar circle with `AD`

The missing functionality is that there is currently no place in the web UI for a user to:
- see their issued tokens
- revoke one of their existing tokens
- issue a new token

And there is currently no place for an admin to manage the global/all-token side of that problem from the web UI.
close_reason: Implemented token management UI and admin token controls
implementation:
# Goal
Provide a usable token-management area in the web UI so users and admins can inspect and manage API tokens without dropping to manual tooling.

# Required behavior
- Users can view their own API tokens/sessions in the web UI.
- Users can revoke their own tokens.
- Users can issue/create a new token from the web UI.
- Admins can access broader token-management capabilities for global/all-token issues.
- The entry point should live under the far-right account/admin dropdown.

# Screenshot details captured in issue
The relevant header area currently contains:
- `Live map`
- `gRPC API`
- `Admin`
- a far-right account control with `admin`, `Admin`, and `AD`
This is the intended location for the token-management entry.

# Design notes
- This should align with the JWT/token lifecycle and revocation model being introduced for the gRPC/auth work.
- If there is no full profile page yet, the dropdown can lead to a dedicated token-management page or modal, but it should be reachable from the far-right account menu.
- The UI should make the difference between user-scoped token management and admin/global actions clear.
- Be careful about how much token material is shown after creation; avoid exposing secrets repeatedly if one-time display is more appropriate.
- Consider whether the token page should show created-at, last-used, scope/type, and revocation status if available.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Define the token-management UX entry under the far-right account/admin dropdown.
2. Add a user-facing page/section for listing and revoking that user's tokens.
3. Add a flow for issuing a new token from the web UI.
4. Add admin-only views/actions for global or all-token management.
5. Align the UI behavior with the backend revocation/token model from the gRPC/auth work.
6. Add tests for user visibility, self-revocation, token issuance, and admin-only actions.

# Acceptance criteria
- Users can reach token management from the far-right account dropdown.
- Users can see their own tokens, revoke them, and issue a new one.
- Admins can access the broader global/all-token management functions.
- The UI clearly distinguishes personal token management from admin/global actions.

title: Replace Nix sourcesJsonFile JSON blob with proper structured Nix options
description:
Replace the current NixOS module pattern of pointing Hrafnsyn at a raw JSON file like:

`sourcesJsonFile = /run/secrets/hrafnsyn-sources.json;`

with a proper structured Nix configuration for defining sources directly in the module.

The current approach is ugly and awkward because operators have to maintain a separate JSON array file instead of using typed Nix options.

Current JSON shape in use:
```json
[
  {
    "id": "planes-main",
    "name": "Airplane SDR",
    "vehicle_type": "plane",
    "adapter": "dump1090",
    "base_url": "http://10.255.101.202",
    "poll_interval_ms": 1000,
    "enabled": true
  },
  {
    "id": "boats-main",
    "name": "Boat SDR",
    "vehicle_type": "vessel",
    "adapter": "ais_catcher",
    "base_url": "http://10.255.101.202:8100",
    "poll_interval_ms": 2500,
    "enabled": true
  }
]
```

The desired outcome is a clean native Nix structure for configuring sources instead of an external JSON blob.
implementation:
# Goal
Make Hrafnsyn's NixOS configuration feel like a proper Nix module by replacing `sourcesJsonFile` with typed structured options for source definitions.

# Required behavior
- Define sources directly in Nix instead of requiring a separate JSON file.
- Replace or de-emphasize `sourcesJsonFile` in favor of a structured option set/list.
- Preserve support for configuring all current source properties:
  - `id`
  - `name`
  - `vehicle_type`
  - `adapter`
  - `base_url`
  - `poll_interval_ms`
  - `enabled`
- Keep the runtime environment generation aligned with the app's existing `HRAFNSYN_SOURCES_JSON` expectation, if that remains the internal boundary.

# Design notes
- The app can still consume JSON internally if useful, but the Nix module should generate that from typed Nix config rather than forcing operators to hand-maintain JSON.
- Prefer proper option typing, validation, defaults, and documentation in the Nix module.
- Consider whether `sourcesJsonFile` should remain as an escape hatch for advanced users or be deprecated entirely.
- Update deployment docs/examples to use the new structured Nix syntax.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Audit the current Nix module option definition and how it maps to `HRAFNSYN_SOURCES_JSON`.
2. Design a structured Nix option schema for sources.
3. Generate the runtime JSON/env from the structured Nix config internally.
4. Update docs and examples to stop recommending the raw JSON file approach.
5. Decide on compatibility/deprecation handling for `sourcesJsonFile`.

# Acceptance criteria
- Operators can define Hrafnsyn sources directly as structured Nix config.
- The module no longer requires a hand-authored JSON file for normal use.
- Source definitions remain fully expressive for current adapters.
- Nix module docs/examples reflect the new preferred configuration pattern.

title: Complete gRPC protocol with JWT auth, refresh, and token revocation
description:
Complete the gRPC protocol implementation so a full app can be built on top of it, including token-based authentication flows for authenticated deployments and sensible behavior for readonly/open deployments.

This work should add JWT access tokens with refresh support and sane expiry defaults. When the system is running with authentication required, clients must be able to log in with username and password to obtain a token. When authentication is not required, clients should be able to use the gRPC API without a login flow first, and JWT validation should not block access.

Users also need token revocation controls: users must be able to revoke their own tokens, and admins must be able to revoke all tokens globally. There is no user profile page yet, so the eventual self-service token management UI should be noted for that future location when implementation begins.
close_reason: Implemented gRPC auth, tracking API, and revocation flows
implementation:
# Goal
Complete the planned gRPC transport so it is viable as the foundation for a full client application, with authentication behavior that matches Hrafnsyn's existing auth modes.

# Required behavior
- Implement the gRPC protocol surface needed for a full app experience, not just a partial transport seam.
- Add JWT-based authentication with both access tokens and refresh tokens.
- Define sane token timeouts/expiry values and document/configure them clearly.
- When auth mode is `required`, provide a login RPC that accepts username + password and returns token material.
- When auth mode is not `required`, do not require login before using the API, and do not reject requests solely because no JWT was provided.
- Support user-scoped token revocation so a user can revoke their own tokens/sessions.
- Support admin-triggered global token revocation across the system.
- Note that there is no user profile page yet; when implementation starts, token self-management UX should be designed to live there later.

# Design notes
- Reuse existing `Hrafnsyn.Accounts` authentication rules where possible instead of creating a parallel auth model.
- Keep the gRPC implementation aligned with `Hrafnsyn.Ingest` and other existing domain boundaries rather than bypassing them.
- Treat token validation behavior as environment/runtime-config aware so authenticated and unauthenticated deployments both behave intentionally.
- Decide whether global revocation is implemented via token versioning, revocation timestamps, a revocation table, or similar server-side invalidation strategy; choose an approach that also supports per-user self-revocation cleanly.
- Refresh token handling should account for rotation, invalidation, and timeout semantics.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Audit the current `proto/hrafnsyn/v1/tracking.proto` seam and server-side gRPC scaffolding to identify missing RPCs/messages for app-complete usage.
2. Define auth-related protobuf messages/RPCs for login, refresh, and revoke operations.
3. Implement server-side JWT issuance and verification with configurable expiry/timeout policy.
4. Wire auth-required vs auth-optional runtime behavior so unauthenticated mode bypasses login/token enforcement intentionally.
5. Implement token revocation paths for self-service user revocation and admin global revocation.
6. Add tests covering:
   - login success/failure in auth-required mode
   - refresh flow and expiry behavior
   - unauthenticated access in auth-optional mode
   - revoked token rejection
   - global revocation invalidating existing sessions/tokens
7. Update docs/config/runtime guidance to describe the gRPC auth modes and token lifecycle.

# Acceptance criteria
- A client can fully authenticate and maintain a session via gRPC when auth is required.
- A client can use the gRPC API without login when auth is not required.
- Refresh tokens work and expired/revoked tokens are handled correctly.
- Users can revoke their own tokens.
- Admins can revoke all tokens globally.
- Runtime/docs/proto changes are kept in sync.

title: Implement auth-required login UI, admin bootstrap provisioning, and profile menu
description:
Implement the basic interactive authentication experience for non-public deployments, along with admin bootstrap provisioning and a minimal account menu.

If Hrafnsyn is not running in public mode, the app should show a simple login screen. Authentication for this flow should be username/password based, not email based, so we do not need a mail provider at all for login. We also need a way to provision initial admin users from configuration. For Nix deployments this should be expressed as `users.\"username\".password = \"...\"`; the configured password values can come from SOPS, and on startup the system should hash and provision those users only if they do not already exist.

Once logged in, the UI should expose a simple profile/avatar icon in the upper-right corner with a dropdown containing at least change password and logout actions.
close_reason: Implemented username-based private auth, bootstrap provisioning, and profile menu
implementation:
# Goal
Add the minimum viable web authentication UX and deployment bootstrap path so private/authenticated Hrafnsyn instances can be used without manual database setup.

# Required behavior
- If the app is not running in public mode, unauthenticated users should be presented with a simple login screen.
- Public mode should continue to bypass the login requirement.
- Authentication should use username + password, not email + password.
- Login should not require any mail provider or email-delivery setup.
- Add bootstrap provisioning for admin-capable users from runtime configuration.
- For NixOS configuration, support a shape like `users."username".password = "..."`.
- Provisioned users should have their configured passwords hashed on startup before persistence.
- Bootstrap provisioning must be idempotent: only create/provision the user if it does not already exist.
- Logged-in users should see a simple upper-right profile/avatar icon.
- The profile dropdown should include at least `Change password` and `Logout`.

# Design notes
- Reuse existing `Hrafnsyn.Accounts` auth logic, password hashing, and session mechanisms where possible, but adapt the user-facing auth path to username-based login rather than email-centric login.
- Keep runtime/env configuration, Phoenix app startup, and Nix module settings in sync.
- Treat bootstrap provisioning as startup-time reconciliation, not ongoing mutation of existing user records.
- Be careful not to overwrite existing passwords or user state for previously created users.
- Avoid introducing a mail dependency for this authentication flow.
- If there is no profile page yet, the dropdown actions can route to minimal dedicated LiveView/pages or modal flows, but the issue should remain scoped to the simplest robust UX.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Audit current auth/public-mode behavior and identify where to gate the dashboard behind login.
2. Adapt the auth/session flow to use username/password rather than email/password for this app.
3. Implement a simple login screen and redirect flow for non-public mode.
4. Add startup provisioning for configured users through the accounts boundary.
5. Extend `config/runtime.exs` and `nix/module.nix` to support bootstrap user configuration for Nix deployments.
6. Ensure configured plaintext passwords are hashed before storage and only used for first-time creation.
7. Add a top-right profile/avatar trigger with a dropdown menu.
8. Implement `Change password` and `Logout` flows with the simplest maintainable UX.
9. Add tests for:
   - login required in non-public mode
   - public mode bypassing login
   - username/password login success/failure
   - bootstrap user creation on startup
   - bootstrap being skipped when user already exists
   - password change flow
   - logout flow
10. Update docs to describe private-mode login behavior and bootstrap user provisioning.

# Acceptance criteria
- Non-public deployments show a login screen to unauthenticated users.
- Public deployments continue to work without login.
- Login uses username/password and does not require email infrastructure.
- Nix/runtime config can provision initial users from configuration.
- Provisioned passwords are hashed and existing users are not overwritten.
- Logged-in users have an upper-right profile menu with change-password and logout actions.
- Runtime, Nix, and docs changes stay aligned.

title: Add names and rename support for API tokens
description: Allow users to name API tokens when issuing them, rename existing tokens later, and surface those names cleanly in the token management UI so sessions are easier to manage.
close_reason: Added named API tokens and rename support

title: Polish API token management UI layout
description: Refine the new token management page so the layout, hierarchy, and responsive behavior feel more intentional and less cluttered.
close_reason: Refined token management layout and visual hierarchy

title: Add local dev auth example config and README setup for non-public mode
description:
Improve local development setup for authentication testing by providing an `.env.example` file and README instructions that make it easy to run Hrafnsyn in non-public mode with a bootstrapped admin user.

The goal is to make auth-related development and testing easy for contributors without having to reverse-engineer runtime settings.

This should include:
- an `.env.example` file with the relevant local-development auth settings
- README instructions describing which property/env setting to change to run the app in non-public mode
- example local bootstrap configuration for an admin/test user with username/password so contributors can log in immediately while developing

The end result should be that someone can switch to non-public mode locally, start the app, and test login/admin flows with a known bootstrapped user.
implementation:
# Goal
Make local auth development easy and repeatable by documenting the required environment toggles and providing an example local configuration for a bootstrapped admin user.

# Required behavior
- Add an `.env.example` file for local development.
- Document in the README how to run the app in non-public mode for auth testing.
- Clearly identify the property/env variable that controls public vs non-public mode.
- Provide an example local bootstrap admin/test user configuration.
- The example user should use username/password auth, not email-based auth.
- The local setup should make it straightforward to test login and admin flows.

# Design notes
- Keep secrets out of version control; `.env.example` should contain safe example values only.
- Align the example config with the intended runtime/Nix/bootstrap user model so local development matches real deployments closely enough.
- README instructions should focus on the minimum steps needed to test auth locally.
- If the app already reads config from another source in development, document that clearly rather than creating a misleading `.env.example` that is never used.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Audit the current runtime config path for public/non-public mode and bootstrap users in development.
2. Add an `.env.example` with safe sample values for local auth testing.
3. Update the README with a short local-auth testing section.
4. Document how to set non-public mode locally.
5. Document how to bootstrap a sample admin/test username/password user locally.
6. Verify the documented flow actually works end-to-end.

# Acceptance criteria
- The repo contains an `.env.example` suitable for local auth testing.
- The README explains how to run the app in non-public mode locally.
- The README explains how to bootstrap a test admin user with username/password.
- A contributor can follow the documented steps to test login/admin flows locally without guessing at config.

title: Publish discoverable gRPC API docs with pretty UI and proto download
description:
Export the gRPC API documentation in a pretty user-facing form and make the `.proto` file directly downloadable, then surface that entry alongside the other two top navigation/home elements: `Live Map` and `Login`.

The goal is discoverability. Users should be able to notice that Hrafnsyn has a gRPC API without digging through source files or deployment docs.

This should include:
- a browsable, human-friendly gRPC API documentation page
- a direct download link for the protobuf definition file
- navigation placement alongside the existing top-level entries `Live Map` and `Login`

The intended outcome is that users can quickly discover, inspect, and start integrating with the API from the app itself.
implementation:
# Goal
Make the gRPC API self-discoverable from the product UI by publishing attractive API documentation and a direct proto download link in a prominent top-level location.

# Required behavior
- Publish a pretty, human-friendly gRPC API documentation page.
- Provide a direct download link for the protobuf definition file used by clients.
- Surface the API/docs entry alongside `Live Map` and `Login` in the top navigation or equivalent top-level UI.
- Make the docs usable for developers who are discovering Hrafnsyn for the first time.

# Design notes
- Treat this as product-facing discoverability, not just a docs-folder update.
- The docs page should explain the API surface in a browsable format rather than only linking raw files.
- The raw `.proto` download should still be available for real client generation workflows.
- Keep this aligned with the actual gRPC implementation so the published docs are not ahead of reality.
- Consider whether the page should describe auth expectations, streaming behavior, and example usage once those parts are implemented.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Choose how to render/publish gRPC docs in a polished format.
2. Expose the current protobuf file as a downloadable artifact.
3. Add a top-level navigation entry near `Live Map` and `Login`.
4. Ensure the docs page and proto download stay in sync with `proto/hrafnsyn/v1/tracking.proto`.
5. Add/update docs and tests as needed for the new route/page.

# Acceptance criteria
- Users can discover the gRPC API from the main top-level navigation.
- The API docs are available in a readable, polished format.
- The `.proto` file can be downloaded directly.
- The published docs reflect the actual API surface in the repo.

title: Make sidebar search jump to and select matching track, and move it above details collapsed by default
description:
Search appears to find or accept known identifiers, but it does not complete the expected navigation/selection behavior. Update the search interaction so selecting a result or submitting a known query moves the map to the matching tracked object, selects it, and shows the hover/details card. Also move the search UI above the details panel and keep it collapsed by default unless it is being used.

In the captured example, the search area shows:
- heading `Search`
- helper text `Boat / plane id, callsign, name`
- input containing `AFR69ZJ`
- notice text `Public access is readonly. Add an admin user later to unlock account management.`
- source/status cards below, including:
  - `PLANE` / `Airplane SDR` / `poll every 1.0s` / `DUMP1090`
  - `VESSEL` / `Boat SDR` / `poll every 2.5s` / `AIS-CATCHER`

The corrected functional problem is not that search is entirely broken. The issue is that searching for something like `AFR69ZJ` does not move the map to the relevant aircraft/vessel, does not select it, and does not show the corresponding hover/details state.
implementation:
# Goal
Make search complete the expected find-and-focus workflow, and reposition it so it is available earlier in the sidebar flow without consuming space when inactive.

# Required behavior
- Search should resolve known identifiers/names as it does now or better.
- Selecting a search result, or submitting a direct unique query, should move the map to the matching tracked object.
- The matching object should become selected.
- The associated hover/details popup or details state should be shown after selection.
- Move the search section above the details panel.
- Keep search collapsed by default unless the user expands it or actively uses it.
- Preserve discoverability while reducing permanent sidebar clutter.

# Screenshot details captured in issue
The current UI shows:
- `Search`
- helper copy `Boat / plane id, callsign, name`
- input value `AFR69ZJ`
- readonly/public-mode notice
- source cards for `Airplane SDR` / `DUMP1090` and `Boat SDR` / `AIS-CATCHER`
The corrected report is that the query can be entered, but the UI does not jump to the matching object and surface its details.

# Design notes
- Audit whether the missing behavior is in query resolution, result selection, map pan/zoom, marker selection state, or details/hover rendering.
- Decide the expected behavior for exact-match direct submission vs choosing from explicit results.
- Consider centering, fitting, or zooming to a sensible level when focusing the selected track.
- Ensure the focus/select action is consistent with ordinary marker clicks so the same details UI appears.
- Consider auto-expanding search when it has focus, a non-empty query, or active results.
- Search should collapse back cleanly when cleared and inactive.
- The moved/collapsed search panel should not interfere with details-panel workflows.
- Keep the interaction simple and keyboard-friendly.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Trace the current search flow from input event to query resolution to any result/selection action.
2. Fix the handoff from search match to map focus and selected-track state.
3. Ensure the same hover/details UI opens as when the user selects a track directly on the map.
4. Move the search module above the details panel in the sidebar layout.
5. Add collapsed-by-default behavior with sensible expansion triggers.
6. Test search with plane IDs, vessel IDs, callsigns, and names, including edge cases where the result is off-screen.

# Acceptance criteria
- Searching for known items such as `AFR69ZJ` focuses the map on the correct object.
- The matching object becomes selected and its hover/details UI is shown.
- Search appears above details in the sidebar.
- Search is collapsed by default when inactive.
- Search expands when used and does not create unnecessary permanent vertical clutter.

title: Add FlightAware outbound links for aircraft photos and live flight pages, and support planned-route access
description:
Investigate and implement two FlightAware-style affordances for aircraft details:
1. an outbound link/button to aircraft photo pages in a new browser tab
2. a way to reach flight-specific pages / planned-route context for the selected aircraft

This should not depend on screenshots or the external pages still being available later, so the observed details and URL patterns are copied here.

Observed FlightAware flight page example from the screenshot:
- flight title `Royal Air Maroc 1632`
- identifiers `RAM1632 / AT1632`
- status `EN ROUTE AND ON TIME`
- route shown as `TTU TETOUAN, MOROCCO` to `MAD MADRID, SPAIN`
- page includes `Flight Details`, `View track log`, and `Track inbound plane`
- the details area shows scheduled/actual departure and arrival timing

Observed FlightAware photo page example from the screenshot:
- aircraft/gallery for registration `CN-RGO`
- photo browsing page with tabs such as `FEATURED`, `ALL PHOTOS`, `RECENT COMMENTS`, `COMMUNITY TAGGING`
- multiple aircraft photos are shown in a grid for that registration

Investigated URL patterns:
- photo gallery pages are under `https://www.flightaware.com/photos/aircraft/<REG>`
- known working examples include `https://www.flightaware.com/photos/aircraft/9HQDA` and `https://www.flightaware.com/photos/aircraft/CNRGO`
- this suggests registrations should be uppercased and normalized by stripping separators like hyphens before building the URL
- the photo URL can still resolve to a page even if photos may not exist for that registration, so the Hrafnsyn UI should treat this as an external lookup rather than assuming image availability
- flight redirect pages can be reached via `https://www.flightaware.com/live/modes/<HEX>/ident/<IDENT>/redirect`
- investigated example: `https://www.flightaware.com/live/modes/4d2257/ident/RYR2LM/redirect`
- that example redirects to a concrete flight-history page: `https://www.flightaware.com/live/flight/RYR1821/history/20260407/1610Z/GMTT/EDLV`

The product goal is not to embed FlightAware content. It is to make it easy from Hrafnsyn to open the relevant external page in a new browser tab, and to improve aircraft context by exposing planned-route / flight-page access where we can identify the needed flight.
implementation:
# Goal
Add lightweight external enrichments for aircraft details: a button to open FlightAware photo galleries for the aircraft registration, and a button/link to open the matching FlightAware flight page or planned-route context when it can be identified.

# Required behavior
- Add an outbound `Photos` action for aircraft that opens the FlightAware aircraft photo page in a new browser tab.
- Build the photos URL from the aircraft registration/tail number using an uppercase, separator-stripped normalization step.
- Do not assume photos exist; this is just an external lookup link.
- Add an outbound `Flight page` / `Planned route` style action when enough data exists to build or resolve a matching FlightAware link.
- Prefer simple outbound navigation rather than scraping or embedding external content in Hrafnsyn.
- If a planned-route feature already exists or is later added locally, make sure this issue aligns with it instead of creating duplicate UX.

# Investigation notes captured here
- FlightAware photo pattern: `/photos/aircraft/<REG>`
- Verified examples:
  - `https://www.flightaware.com/photos/aircraft/9HQDA`
  - `https://www.flightaware.com/photos/aircraft/CNRGO`
- This strongly suggests `9H-QDA -> 9HQDA` and `CN-RGO -> CNRGO` normalization.
- A likely no-match photo URL still returns a normal page shell rather than a hard failure, so the integration should not promise that photos exist.
- FlightAware redirect pattern: `/live/modes/<HEX>/ident/<IDENT>/redirect`
- Verified example:
  - `https://www.flightaware.com/live/modes/4d2257/ident/RYR2LM/redirect`
  - redirects to `https://www.flightaware.com/live/flight/RYR1821/history/20260407/1610Z/GMTT/EDLV`
- This suggests Hrafnsyn may be able to construct a useful external flight-page link when it has a Mode S hex and a plausible ident/callsign, though exact resolution behavior should be validated during implementation.

# Design notes
- Scope this as an outbound-link enhancement first, not a deep third-party integration.
- The `Photos` action likely belongs in the aircraft details card or selected-track details area.
- The flight-page / planned-route action should only render when the aircraft has enough identifying data to make the link useful.
- Planned route in Hrafnsyn is distinct from the existing observed historical route overlay; avoid confusing observed track history with scheduled/planned routing.
- If later work introduces native planned-route data, this issue should either reuse that area or be linked to it.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Identify where aircraft registration, hex, and callsign/ident are available in the selected-track UI.
2. Add a normalized helper for FlightAware photo URLs based on aircraft registration.
3. Add a `Photos` button that opens the external gallery in a new tab.
4. Evaluate how reliably a FlightAware redirect URL can be built from current track data.
5. Add a `Flight page` or `Planned route` action when sufficient data is present.
6. Decide how to present unavailable/insufficient-data cases without cluttering the UI.
7. If planned-route support belongs in a separate native feature, create or link that follow-up during implementation.

# Acceptance criteria
- Aircraft with a registration can offer a `Photos` button that opens the normalized FlightAware gallery URL in a new tab.
- The UI does not assume photos exist for that aircraft.
- Aircraft with sufficient flight identifiers can offer a `Flight page` / planned-route external action.
- The feature is framed as optional external enrichment, not guaranteed local data.
- Observed-route UI and planned-route/external-flight-page UI are clearly differentiated.

title: Tighten login notifications and make the top-right admin menu stay usable
description:
Fix two related authentication/header UX issues:

1. Notifications shown around login linger too long before disappearing.
2. The top-right admin/profile menu does not stay open long enough to be usable when clicking the far-right admin/avatar area.

In the captured header example, the top navigation shows:
- `Live map`
- `gRPC API`
- `Admin`
- a far-right profile/admin control showing `admin`, `Admin`, and an avatar circle with `AD`

The reported behavior is:
- login-related notifications stay on screen too long and feel sticky
- clicking the far-right admin/admin/avatar area opens a menu that does not hang around long enough to interact with
close_reason: Fixed login flash timing and profile menu usability
implementation:
# Goal
Improve the login/header interaction quality by making notifications feel timely and making the admin/profile menu reliably usable.

# Required behavior
- Login-related notifications should dismiss sooner or otherwise feel less sticky.
- The far-right admin/profile menu should remain open long enough for a user to move the pointer and click items.
- The menu interaction should feel predictable for both click and hover transitions if hover is involved.

# Screenshot details captured in issue
The header shows:
- `Live map`
- `gRPC API`
- `Admin`
- a far-right account control with `admin`, `Admin`, and an `AD` avatar badge
The specific complaint is that notifications linger too long and the far-right admin menu disappears too quickly to use.

# Design notes
- Audit whether notifications use a fixed timeout that is simply too long.
- Consider whether login success toasts should auto-dismiss faster than other message types.
- Audit whether the admin menu is hover-driven, click-driven, or losing focus because of pointer gaps.
- Prefer a menu interaction that is resilient to small pointer movements and works well on both desktop and touch/click usage.
- If needed, add a short close delay, pointer-safe hover area, or explicit click-to-toggle behavior.

# Suggested implementation slices
1. Identify the login notification/toast implementation and current dismissal timing.
2. Tune notification behavior so login messages clear in a more reasonable amount of time.
3. Identify why the admin/profile menu closes prematurely.
4. Adjust the menu trigger/dropdown behavior to make it reliably usable.
5. Test the top-right menu with realistic pointer movement between trigger and menu items.

# Acceptance criteria
- Login notifications no longer linger excessively.
- The top-right admin/profile menu can be opened and used reliably.
- The menu does not disappear before a user can move into it and click an item.
